canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, WSC (Winograd Schema Challenge) is predominantly characterized as a benchmark for coreference resolution that requires pronoun disambiguation. The task is operationalized through multiple-choice questions, as noted in several sources. A recurring theme is that solving these questions necessitates commonsense reasoning, with some papers specifying "deep commonsense reasoning." While its primary application is measuring `Coreference resolution`, WSC is also used to evaluate `Commonsense understanding`. A smaller line of work also uses it to assess `In-context and few-shot learning`. One paper frames it more broadly as a general "reasoning benchmark" for evaluating downstream model performance.
papers: CoachMe: Decoding Sport Elements with a Reference-Based Coaching Instruction Generation Model
Improving Dialogue State Tracking through Combinatorial Search for In-Context Examples
MultiSocial: Multilingual Benchmark of Machine-Generated Text Detection of Social-Media Texts

Enhancing Zeroth-order Fine-tuning for Language Models with Low-rank Structures
Neuron-based Multifractal Analysis of Neuron Interaction Dynamics in Large Models
PolyPythias: Stability and Outliers across Fifty Language Model Pre-Training Runs
Second-Order Fine-Tuning without Pain for LLMs: A Hessian Informed Zeroth-Order Optimizer
Streamlining Redundant Layers to Compress Large Language Models
Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning of LLMs with Transferable Static Sparsity
Occult: Optimizing Collaborative Communications across Experts for Accelerated Parallel MoE Training and Inference
From Distributional to Overton Pluralism: Investigating Large Language Model Alignment
Teaching Models to Balance Resisting and Accepting Persuasion
